hook: A museum built entirely from the objects and testimonies of children who grew up under the siege.
facts: Opened in January 2017 at Logavina 32 in the old town, founded by Sarajevan author and 'war child' Jasminko Halilovic.
Grew out of a 2010 online project collecting siege childhood memories from over 1,000 young adults, published as a book in 2013.
Holds over 3,000 donated objects (toys, diaries, clothing, photographs) each paired with the donor's personal account, plus 60+ recorded oral histories.
Won the 2018 Council of Europe Museum Prize, one of the museum sector's most prestigious awards.
Open daily (except 1 January), roughly 11:00-19:00; nearest tram stops are Baščaršija and Katedrala.
w: It began as a question posted online — what a war childhood meant — and grew into a museum of small things: a ballet shoe, a homemade sled, a diary, each with the child's own caption. Jasminko Halilovic's collection turns the siege into something you grasp through toys and scribbles, not statistics. Quiet, and devastating.

n: Tunnel of Hope (Sarajevo War Tunnel Museum)
hook: The hand-dug tunnel under the besieged airport runway that was the city's only lifeline to the outside world.
facts: Dug between 1 March and 30 June 1993 by the Army of the Republic of Bosnia and Herzegovina under deputy commander General Rašid Zorlak, opening to use on 1 July 1993.
The full structure ran about 840 metres: 340 m of actual tunnel under the airport runway plus covered trenches on the Dobrinja and Butmir sides.
Its Butmir entrance was hidden inside the Kolar family house at Tuneli 1, which now forms the museum.
The museum shows an 18-minute film, war photographs, uniforms and equipment; visitors can walk about 20 metres of the preserved original tunnel.
It is now one of Sarajevo's most-visited sites, open daily (seasonal hours roughly 08:30-17:30 in summer).
w: Under the airport runway, dug by hand in 1993 while the city starved, ran 800 metres of timber-propped tunnel. Soldiers, food, fuel and hope all passed through this single lifeline beneath UN-controlled tarmac. A house at the Butmir end still hides the entrance; you can stoop into a surviving stretch. The siege's back door.

hook: The covered market where two mortar massacres struck queues of civilians, now marked by a memorial and a resin-filled Sarajevo Rose.
facts: The first Markale massacre, on 5 February 1994, killed 68 people and wounded 144 with a single 120 mm mortar shell.
The second massacre, on 28 August 1995, killed 43 and wounded 75 with five mortar shells; it helped trigger NATO's Operation Deliberate Force.
A red memorial wall lists the victims' names, and the shell-impact scar is among the craters marked as 'Sarajevo Roses' filled with red resin.
The market sits on Mula Mustafe Bašeskije street, on the northern edge of the Baščaršija old town.
Victims are commemorated annually on the anniversary dates at the market.
w: Twice the mortars found this market, in 1994 and again in 1995, killing over a hundred shoppers and helping pull the world into the war at last. A red-backed wall of names now marks the dead, and the scarred paving is filled with crimson resin: a Sarajevo Rose, one of many blooming where shells struck. Look down as you walk.

n: Jewish Museum of Bosnia and Herzegovina (Old Synagogue)
hook: The country's oldest synagogue, the Sephardic 'Stari hram', now a museum of four centuries of Bosnian Jewish life.
facts: Housed in the Old Synagogue (Stari hram) built in 1581 by Siyavush Pasha in the Velika Avlija (Great Courtyard) of Baščaršija, the oldest synagogue in Bosnia.
Has operated as a branch of the Museum of Sarajevo since 1966.
The permanent exhibition spans over 400 years of Bosnian Jewish history with religious artifacts, manuscripts, photographs and traditional clothing.
Holds Ladino and other Jewish books, some printed two to three centuries ago.
Located on Velika avlija / Mula Mustafe Bašeskije area of the old town, near the New Synagogue (Novi hram).
w: Sarajevo's Sephardim arrived after the 1492 expulsion from Spain and built this, the Old Synagogue, around 1581 — the oldest in Bosnia. Today it holds the museum of a community that gave the city its Ladino songs and the priceless Sarajevo Haggadah, and was all but erased in the Holocaust. Four centuries of a people, under one roof.

n: Museum of Crimes Against Humanity and Genocide 1992-1995
hook: A documentary museum of the Bosnian war's atrocities, built around personal items exhumed from mass graves.
facts: Opened in July 2016 at Ferhadija 17, on a pedestrian street in the heart of the old town.
Displays personal effects exhumed from mass graves, including shirts, combs and watches.
Draws on material from the archives of the International Criminal Tribunal for the Former Yugoslavia (ICTY).
Documents siege and genocide events of 1992-1995, including Srebrenica.
Open daily, with long visiting hours (roughly 09:00-22:00).
w: On Ferhadija, behind an unassuming door, a museum lays out the 1992-95 war without flinching: survivor testimony, camp photographs, objects from mass graves and the evidence behind the genocide rulings. It is hard going by design, built so the atrocities cannot be tidied away or denied. Bear witness, then step back into the street.

hook: A Viennese Secession postal palace on the Miljacka, the first building torched in the siege and faithfully rebuilt.
facts: Designed by Austro-Hungarian architect Josip Vancaš in the Viennese Secession style and opened in 1913.
Destroyed by fire on 2 May 1992 when attacked by the Yugoslav People's Army / Army of Republika Srpska, an early move to cut Sarajevo's communications.
Reconstruction led by architect Ferhad Mulabegović was completed in 2001, restoring it as headquarters of BH Pošta.
Known for its glass roof lighting a vast main hall and floral bas-reliefs.
Stands at Obala Kulina bana 8, on the river about 200 m upstream of the Festina Lente footbridge.
w: The grandest room in Austro-Hungarian Sarajevo, this Secession-era post office opened in 1913 with a glass-roofed hall of marble. Serb shelling gutted it in 1992; the restoration that followed became a point of civic pride, the counters and skylight brought back to their old splendour. Buy a stamp under the loveliest ceiling in town.

hook: A vast Sephardic necropolis on the Trebević slope, second-largest in Europe, that became a front-line gun position in the siege.
facts: Established in 1630 by Sephardic Jews during the Ottoman period, on the slopes of Trebević in the Kovačići-Debelo Brdo area.
Covers about 31,160 square metres with more than 3,850 tombstones, the largest Jewish cemetery in Southeast Europe and second-largest in Europe after Prague.
Used until 1966; also became a burial ground for Ashkenazi Jews after they arrived under Austro-Hungarian rule.
Sat on the siege front line and was used as an artillery position; an estimated 95% of tombstones were damaged.
Heavily mined during the war and completely demined in 1996; on Bosnia's UNESCO tentative list.
w: Tumbling down the Trebevic slope stand thousands of pale, boxy Sephardic tombstones, unlike any others in Europe — the second-largest Jewish cemetery on the continent, in use from 1630. During the siege the graves became a frontline, sown with mines and lined with snipers. Cleared and quiet now, the stones lean downhill toward the city.

hook: A giant steel tin of beef, the city's deadpan 'thank you' for the barely-edible aid sent during the siege.
facts: A painted-steel sculpture of an ICAR beef can on a marble plinth, designed by Nebojša Šerić Šoba and dedicated on 6 April 2007.
Stands on a walking path behind the Historical Museum of Bosnia and Herzegovina, toward the Miljacka in Marijin Dvor.
Its plinth reads 'Monument to the International Community by the grateful citizens of Sarajevo', an ironic dedication.
Commemorates the canned food aid distributed by international organisations during the siege of 1992-1995.
ICAR canned beef became a siege-era symbol of foreign aid that residents found largely unpalatable.
legend: Siege lore held the canned beef was sometimes leftover, decades-expired military rations, and that even Sarajevo's cats and dogs refused to eat it.
w: A giant tin of corned beef, cast in bronze and set on a plinth like a war hero — the city's sardonic thank-you for the inedible aid rations that kept it alive. Sarajevans loathed the mystery ICAR beef but ate it anyway through the siege. Unveiled in 2007, the monument salutes survival with a straight face and a very dark joke.

hook: The graffiti-drenched concrete serpent of the 1984 Winter Olympics, later a wartime artillery position, now snaking through the Trebević forest.
facts: Built for the 1984 Sarajevo Winter Olympics; track assembly was completed on 30 September 1982 at a cost of YUD 563,209,000.
It ran 1,300 m for the bobsleigh and 1,210 m for the luge.
At the 1984 Games it drew 30,000 bobsleigh spectators and 20,000 luge spectators.
During the 1992-1995 Siege of Sarajevo the track was used as an artillery position by Bosnian Serb forces and was heavily damaged.
After the war it became a canvas for local graffiti artists and an informal cycling/hiking route; partial restoration (overgrowth and graffiti removal, protective coating) began in June 2014.
It is now reachable via the rebuilt Trebević cable car and is one of Sarajevo's best-known abandoned attractions.
w: Olympic sledders hit 90 km/h down this concrete chute in 1984; eight years later it served as a Serb artillery position, its curves notched with gun ports. Now the snaking track climbs through the Trebevic forest under a skin of graffiti, walkable end to end. Glory, war and spray paint, poured into one ribbon of concrete.

hook: An Austro-Hungarian hilltop fort reborn as Bosnia's only astronomical observatory, then shelled to a crumbling shell during the siege.
facts: The site began as an Austro-Hungarian military fortification (Bistrik Kula) on Mount Trebević.
After losing its military role post-WWII, it was entrusted in the 1960s to the Orion Astronomical Society, which built the observatory between 1969 and 1972.
It was the only astronomical observatory of its kind in Bosnia and Herzegovina, with three domes (one eight metres in diameter), a library and a photo lab.
The observatory and all its instruments were destroyed during the Bosnian War (1992-1995) and the site was subsequently abandoned.
The crumbling white building still stands over the city, with decrepit, collapsing walls.
w: An Austro-Hungarian fort on the Trebevic ridge became, in 1969, the only astronomical observatory in Bosnia — until the siege wrecked it and looters stripped the rest. Its cracked dome and empty rooms now stare out over the valley, stars traded for a sweeping view of the city that ruined it. Climb up for the silence and the shell.

hook: An abandoned 1984 Olympic-era hotel turned Bosnian Serb sniper nest, still rotting on a disputed strip of mountain no garbage company will claim.
facts: Hotel Osmica opened in 1984 and was a popular haunt of musicians, intellectuals and athletes during Sarajevo's Olympic era; its name comes from 'osmica' (figure-eight), the nickname for the twisting road up Trebević.
When the Siege of Sarajevo began, Bosnian Serb forces (VRS) seized the hotel and used it as a sniper position, exploiting its nearly unobstructed view down into the city.
It sits near the inter-entity boundary line; the line in front of the hotel was reportedly never precisely fixed.
Because of that boundary ambiguity, neither local garbage-disposal company claims the site, leaving it both abandoned and continuously littered.
The empty interior remains explorable; as of April 2025 the ruin was reported unchanged.
w: Built for the 1984 Games, this mountain hotel ended the decade as a Serb firing position, its picture windows turned on the city below. It was never rebuilt: stranded on the line between Sarajevo and Republika Srpska, it rots roofless in the trees, walls layered in graffiti. The view that sold the rooms now belongs to anyone who hikes up.

hook: The world's last clock tower still keeping Ottoman lunar time, hand-set each week so noon falls at sunset.
facts: Standing beside the Gazi Husrev-beg Mosque in Baščaršija, it is the tallest of Bosnia's 21 clock towers at about 30 m.
It was erected by Gazi Husrev-beg, the Ottoman-era governor; the earliest documented mention is by Evliya Çelebi in the 17th century.
It uniquely keeps lunar ('a la turka') time: the hands read 12 o'clock at the moment of sunset, the time of the Maghrib prayer.
All other clock towers in Bosnia and Herzegovina were converted to European time; only Sarajevo's still runs on lunar time.
A caretaker called the muvakkit ('timekeeper') resets the clock by hand once a week.
Its clock mechanism was acquired from London in 1875; the tower was declared a National Monument of Bosnia and Herzegovina in 2006.
w: The hands of this stone tower keep no ordinary time: they read a la turca, striking twelve at sunset so the faithful know the hour of Maghrib prayer. Reckoned the only clock still set to lunar time, it must be reset by hand each week by a muvekkit. Beside the Gazi Husrev-beg mosque, it has counted Sarajevo's evenings since the 1600s.

hook: The house an old man made the Habsburgs move stone by numbered stone across the Miljacka, rather than surrender it for City Hall — now a restaurant glaring back at Vijećnica.
facts: When Austria-Hungary planned the Vijećnica (City Hall) in the late 19th century, a private house stood on the chosen riverbank plot.
The owner refused to let it be demolished and agreed only on the condition that it be dismantled and rebuilt, identically, on the opposite bank of the Miljacka.
Every component was numbered and carried across the river, and the Austro-Hungarian administration reconstructed it on the left bank with the original materials.
The rebuilt house was named Inat Kuća, the 'House of Spite', a symbol of Bosnian stubbornness (inat).
It was converted into a traditional Bosnian restaurant in 1997 and stands at Veliki Alifakovac 1, directly opposite City Hall.
legend: Local lore says the owner, known as old Benderija, spent every day of the move sitting in the middle of a nearby bridge, smoking and watching the workers carry each brick across the river.
w: When the Austro-Hungarians wanted this old Ottoman house cleared to raise the City Hall, its owner agreed on one condition: that every stone be carried across the Miljacka and rebuilt on the far bank. They did. 'Inat' — pure spite — won the house a riverside plot and an immortal name. Now a restaurant, glaring at the grand hall opposite.

hook: Tanks, jeeps and artillery rusting in the grounds behind the Historical Museum, where café-goers at Caffe Tito let their kids clamber over the war machines.
facts: The Historical Museum of Bosnia and Herzegovina, on Zmaja od Bosne, keeps WWI and WWII military artefacts standing in its outdoor grounds.
The open-air display includes tanks, jeeps and other military hardware in the museum's yard.
Tucked directly behind the museum is Caffe Tito, a Yugoslav-nostalgia café decorated with Tito-era memorabilia, at Zmaja od Bosne 5.
Visitor accounts describe children playing on the museum's military vehicles while adults sit on Caffe Tito's terrace.
The site sits along Wilson's Promenade (Vilsonovo šetalište) in the Marijin Dvor area.
w: Behind the brutalist Historical Museum, a yard of old tanks, field guns and a UN jeep sits open to the weather, climbed on by kids and ringed by the parasols of Caffe Tito. Yugoslav-era hardware and siege relics share the grass with a bar that toasts the old marshal. Order a coffee, then go clamber on a tank. Only in Sarajevo.

hook: Maximalist antique-crammed bar off Ferhadija where every inch is mirrors, goldfish tanks and vintage trinkets.
facts: The name translates as 'Golden Fish' / 'Goldfish'; the interior keeps live goldfish tanks among the decor.
Located at Kaptol 5, a quiet side street near the Ferhadija pedestrian strip and the city centre.
Interior is packed in 'organized chaos' with coins, needlework, framed photos, postcards, love letters and old festival posters.
Staff are often dressed in period-style clothing, adding to the theatrical, antique-shop atmosphere.
Widely cited as one of Sarajevo's most famous bars and frequently listed among Europe's most distinctive bars.
Cash-only (does not accept cards) and photography inside is discouraged/forbidden.
w: Push past the velvet and you are inside a magpie's hoard: chandeliers, stuffed birds, ticking clocks, banknotes pinned to the walls and a goldfish presiding over the till. Zlatna Ribica pours its drinks with a complimentary shot of something herbal and a trinket from a drawer. Cash only, no photos, and one of the strangest bars anywhere.

hook: A former art cinema reborn as a raw, smoke-filled underground bar famous for its Monday-night sevdah singalongs.
facts: Set in the lobby of a former/old cinema at Alipašina 19 in the Centar municipality.
Operates as an alternative cafe-bar; tagged as an active pub in OpenStreetMap.
Its signature night is the Monday 'Sevdah Soiree', when musicians with guitars, accordions and drums move among the packed tables playing sevdalinkas and old city songs.
Draws a mixed crowd of locals, students and travellers seeking an authentic, low-key Sarajevo night out.
Confirmed still operating, with visitor reviews continuing into 2025-2026.
w: A faded 1940s cinema on Alipasina, its screen long dark, reborn as the city's most beloved dive. The old stalls fill on Monday nights for raucous live sevdah, the crowd bawling along to the melancholy songs over cheap beer and smoke. Threadbare, loud and entirely without pretension. The films stopped; the show never did.

hook: Old-town specialty coffee house pairing traditional Bosnian coffee ritual with modern espresso drinks.
facts: Main branch sits at Kovači 26 in the Stari Grad (old town), in the Kovači neighbourhood near Baščaršija.
Serves both traditional Bosnian coffee (and salep/baklava) and specialty espresso-based drinks such as lattes.
Its name puns on 'ćejf' — the Bosnian notion of unhurried, mindful pleasure, especially around coffee.
Listed by Sarajevo tourism and Baščaršija guides as a destination coffee spot in the old town.
Phone listed as +387 61 482 036; the brand has more than one outlet, with Kovači 26 as the principal/old-town location.
w: Named for cejf, the Bosnian art of taking unhurried pleasure in small things, this Kovaci coffee bar treats a cup as a ceremony rather than a transaction. Specialty roasts, a view up toward the martyrs' cemetery, and cake worth lingering over. Sit, sip, and let the morning go nowhere in particular. The whole philosophy, in a demitasse.

hook: Intimate cellar club for live jazz, blues and rock, hidden in a passage off Maršala Tita street.
facts: Located at Maršala Tita 38 (node 38d) in central Sarajevo, in a passage behind the McDonald's.
Roughly 120 m² club programming live jazz, blues, rock, funk and DJ nights.
Mondays are dedicated to jazz and Tuesdays to blues, with the wider week covering rock and DJ sets.
A platform for both established Bosnian performers and young regional bands testing the stage.
Open through the week and repeatedly cited as one of the best live-music venues in the city.
w: Down a stair off Marsala Tita, a low brick cellar fills nightly with jazz, blues and whatever local band has the room — the kind of place where the music starts late and the audience knows the players. Sarajevo has always lived for its soundtrack, and Jazzbina keeps the smoky, close-pressed version of it alive. Find a seat near the front.

hook: Colourful patisserie-cafe known for some of the best cakes in Sarajevo and a playful book-lined interior.
facts: Main location is at Trg djece Dobrinje 9 in the Dobrinja district of Novi Grad municipality.
A 'slastičarna' is a Bosnian cake-and-pastry cafe; Kup is singled out for its colourful, modern, ornament-filled interior (including books).
Frequently rated among Sarajevo's best dessert cafes by local-tip guides.
The brand has grown to several outlets (e.g. BBI Center and Bulevar Meše Selimovića) plus an international branch, but the Dobrinja shop is the original/colourful one travel writers describe.
Coordinate corroborated by OpenStreetMap as 'Caffé slastičarna Kup, 9, Trg Djece Dobrinje'.
w: The cakes are good; the room is something else — a riot of colour and kitsch out in Dobrinja, every surface painted, tiled or hung with something. Slasticarna Kup turns a stop for baklava and a macchiato into a visual event. Locals have queued at the original shop for years, long before the mall branches. Sugar, served with maximalism.

hook: Beloved local bakery chain famed for its apple strudel and burek, a perennial Sarajevo grab-and-go.
facts: Among Sarajevo's best-known bakeries, especially celebrated for its apple strudel (and burek/pastries).
Operates several branches across the city; this central branch is at Triglavska 1 (Hrasno area), just off the Zmaja od Bosne corridor.
Some branches operate long/24-hour hours, reflecting its role as a neighbourhood meeting point.
Sells the full Bosnian bakery range — bread, burek and other pies (pita), pastries and pizza.
Coordinate for the Triglavska 1 branch corroborated by OpenStreetMap (Pekara 'AS', 1 Triglavska, Hrasno Brdo).
w: The morning queue tells you everything: Pekara AS is where Sarajevo lines up for warm burek and flaky strudel before work. No frills, no seating to speak of, just trays of pastry going out as fast as they come from the oven, wrapped in paper and eaten on the hoof. A daily ritual more than a bakery. Get there early or miss out.

hook: Historic 1880s brewery in Bistrik whose spring water kept besieged Sarajevo alive, now with an on-site pub and museum.
facts: Founded in 1881 when Viennese businessman Heinrich Löwe built a brewery in Bistrik over a major high-quality spring.
In 1893 the Bistrik, Kovačići and Lukavica breweries merged into Dionička Pivara Sarajevo, becoming one of the largest in the Austro-Hungarian Empire.
During the 1992-95 siege the brewery's springs supplied drinking water to residents when rivers were polluted and creeks ran dry.
While people queued for that water the site was shelled hundreds of times, and dozens of Sarajevans were killed or wounded waiting in line.
The on-site Sarajevo Brewery Museum, opened in 2004, is the only brewery museum in Bosnia and Herzegovina, and the complex includes a large pub/restaurant.
Coordinate corroborated by OpenStreetMap as 'Sarajevska pivara, 15, Bistrik'.
w: Founded in 1864 over a natural spring, the Bistrik brewery did something extraordinary during the siege: when the taps ran dry citywide, its underground water became a public lifeline, and Sarajevans queued under sniper fire to fill cans here. The grand red-brick halls still brew, with a beer hall and a small museum. Drink to survival.

hook: A terraced socialist hillside estate so steep it has the only funicular in Sarajevo, sparing residents 414 steps.
facts: Ciglane's terraced housing estate (Naselje Đuro Đaković) was designed by architect Radovan Delalle and built in stages during the late Yugoslav era.
The neighbourhood is named after a brick factory (ciglana) opened by August Braun in 1879 that operated on the site until 1973.
The hillside complex is split into three tiers of brutalist concrete blocks that step up the slope of Crni Vrh hill.
The Kosi Lift ('slanted lift') is the only funicular in Sarajevo, letting residents bypass 414 steep steps; a single ride costs 0.5 convertible marks.
Wikidata records the funicular's official opening date as 18 May 1990.
The estate's concrete walls have become canvases for graffiti, and the district hosts an active flea market.
w: A cascade of terraced concrete housing tumbles down the old brickworks hill, stitched together by Sarajevo's only funicular — the Kosi Lift, which spares residents 414 steps. Radovan Delalle's 1980s estate ranks among the city's boldest socialist architecture, half town, half machine. Ride the little lift to see how it lives.

hook: A monumental socialist terminal begun by Czechoslovak architects and finished by locals after the Tito–Stalin split.
facts: The current station was built between 1947 and 1953, replacing an 1882 station damaged in the Second World War.

An initial 1947 design came from a Czechoslovak team led by Bedřich Hacar; after the 1948 Tito–Stalin split the foreign team withdrew and local architects (Jahiel Finci, Muhamed Kadić, Emanuel Šamanek, Dušan Smiljanić) completed it.
The building has a semi-circular ground plan roofed with reinforced-concrete hyperbolic-paraboloid (shell) structures.
It was conceived as a showpiece of Eastern Bloc architecture, intended to symbolise Sarajevo's integration into Yugoslavia's unified rail network.
Bosnia and Herzegovina designated the station a national monument in 2016.
It stands on the square now named Trg žrtava genocida u Srebrenici, in the Marijin Dvor area northwest of the old centre.
w: Begun by a Czechoslovak team in 1947 and finished by locals after Tito's split with Stalin cut the help short, the station stands as a slab of Yugoslav modernist ambition. Its great shell-concrete canopy once sheltered crowds bound across the federation; today the cavernous hall feels half-empty. Travel dreams, mostly silent now.

hook: A quiet six-block lane in the old town that an American reporter turned into a portrait of the Sarajevo siege.
facts: Logavina is a tertiary street in the Stari Grad (Old Town) municipality, on the upper edge of the Baščaršija quarter's tangle of lanes.
Journalist Barbara Demick's book 'Logavina Street: Life and Death in a Sarajevo Neighborhood' (1996) follows the street's roughly 240 families through the 1992–1995 siege.
Demick spent much of 1993–1995 reporting from the street; the resulting work won the George Polk and Robert F. Kennedy awards and was a Pulitzer finalist.
The book frames the street as a microcosm of pre-war Sarajevo, where Muslims, Serbs and Croats lived side by side.
The street sits within walking distance of Baščaršija, the Ottoman-era bazaar quarter of narrow café-and-souvenir alleys.
w: One ordinary uphill street above the bazaar, half a mile long, became the lens for Barbara Demick's book on the siege — five families on Logavina, dodging snipers for bread. Walk it now past Ottoman houses and small mosques and the war recedes, but the names and doorways are the same. An everyday lane that history happened to choose.

hook: A riverside park that was once an execution ground and a horse market before becoming a green promenade with a music pavilion.
facts: At Mejdan sits on the left bank of the Miljacka River, between the Latinska Ćuprija and Ćumurija bridges, on Hamdije Kreševljakovića street.
In the 15th century the site was a place of public execution, originally named Sijaset Mejdan.
By the mid-15th century it became a horse market, giving it the name At Mejdan ('at' = horse, 'mejdan' = square).
Alija Bakr-baba built a mosque on the site in the 16th century, later with a school, making it a centre of education and culture.
The area was turned into a park between the two world wars; it covers roughly 2,500 m².
Its music pavilion was designed by Josip Pospišil and built in 1913, destroyed in the Second World War and fully rebuilt in 2004.
w: Leafy and gentle now, this riverside park spent the Ottoman centuries as Sijaset Mejdan, the city's execution ground, and later a horse market. A pretty music pavilion from 1913, rebuilt in 2004, stands where crowds once gathered for grimmer reasons. Children feed pigeons on ground that has seen the worst. Sarajevo, layered as ever.

hook: A small riverside park by the Malaysia–Bosnia Friendship Bridge built around a sculpture of two lovers fused into one body.
facts: The park lies in the Čengić Vila neighbourhood of Novo Sarajevo municipality, beside the Malaysian–Bosnian Friendship Bridge over the Miljacka.
At its centre is a sculpture of two embracing lovers merged into a single figure.
The Friendship Bridge it adjoins connects Bulevar Meša Selimovića with Gradačačka Street, linking Čengić Vila and Hrasno; the nearest stop is the Čengić Vila tram station about 140 m away.
The park is fitted with outdoor exercise and street-workout equipment and is popular with couples and families.
legend: Local lore holds it as a meeting spot where couples come to make up after quarrels and where friendships are said to turn into romance; 'Nađemo se kod Ljubavnog' ('let's meet at Lovers') is a common phrase.
w: A small riverside park in Novo Sarajevo, watched over by a sculpture of two figures fused into one, beside the Malaysia-Bosnia friendship bridge. Local lore promises that those who wander its paths together will fall, or stay, in love. No grand monument, just a neighbourhood green with a tender reputation. Bring someone.

hook: A scarce patch of inner-city green that residents fought to save from being paved into a building and car park.
facts: The park is in the Marijin Dvor area of Centar municipality, close to the Magribija mosque.
It was long known as Hastahana, from the Turkish word for hospital, reflecting an Ottoman-era medical use of the site.
In April 2019 Centar's local council approved redeveloping the site with a building and underground parking while keeping about 90% green, triggering 2019–2020 citizen protests.
The protest movement organised under the slogan 'Jedan grad, jedna borba' ('One town, one fight').
Campaigners argued Sarajevo has only about 1.5% designated green space, making such parks worth defending.
From 2005 the park held German artist Helmut Lutz's 'Star Trek' sculpture installation, which was removed in August 2020 ahead of construction.
w: Known to locals as Hastahana, this pocket of green at Marijin Dvor became a battleground in 2019, when residents camped out to stop it being paved for parking. 'One city, one struggle,' they chanted, and won. Now the saved lawns and old trees draw families and chess players. Proof a neighbourhood can keep its breathing space.
